item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the remaining image could not be sourced, and thus is assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that a recent edit was reverted, as it was an unnecessary stylistic change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm six months late. But this thought experiment seems fun, so what the heck?

The metallic cube experiment established that for simple objects the molecules stay standard size and multiply to fill the increased mass.
So we don't have to worry what the rate of decay for a 4x larger and presumably much more unstable plutonium atom would be.

The room temperature critical mass of a sphere of plutonium 239 is 10 kg, so says Google.
Density and shape remain the same when enlarged. So that's another complication avoided.

So if our starting mass is a ball 2.5 kg or more, we get an instant nuclear detonation. Roughly 150% the size of the Nagasaki bomb, if I've got the scaling factors right.1

Anything less than 2.5 kg starter would yield "only" an incredibly dangerous radioactive ball.

But then we'd still have an 8% chance of "enlargement decay", like the silver coins. We don't have specific figures on the radiation levels, but it changed atomically stable silver to something with a half life of about 10 hours. So it's safe to say the radioactivity increase is "significant".

If our just-barely sub-critical resized mass hits this glitch, the net effect is the same as transmuting the plutonium to some much higher number element. The critical mass point on this new pseudo-element would be much lower. So we'd almost certainly get a nuclear blast all out of proportion to the limits of what 9 kg of any natural element should produce.

Conclusion: Experimental proposal rejected by site command.

Footnotes
1. I probably don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So what if we placed a subcritical mass of pure plutonium and cranked it up to 4.00?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept of this skip, and the test log is pretty cool. But why a kitchen timer? I know that this is from the early days of SCP where it was basically "anything can be anomalous lol," but I never understood why they made it a kitchen timer instead of something else, like a microwave or a toaster.

Of course, this doesn't hamper me from upvoting it. Just a small question.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Experiment SCP-1056-█
Object: SCP-682
Setting: 0.██﻿
Result: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0Q_1KW0Q-10

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was the second Factory SCP I read (the first was the 110% sticker one), and this was also good, with, as said by others above already, well-thought out about how the device functions to alter the physiology of the objects it is used on. I read this without the logs and already got a good impression; reading them now only improved its weirdness further. Nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. I really like the details put into it. I wonder how those high school students got a hold of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Can't we use it on 682?" Great job. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I sent you a PM, but you havn't replied yet.
I am almost certain that the image is a photoshopped version of this stock image: http://ecx.images-amazon.com/images/I/41NYFGX7B7L._SX450_.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The images in this article were removed because we could not properly determine whether or not the images were properly licensed or attributed. They may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact anqxyr or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this one. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There had obviously been some thought spent on how the SCP changes things. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The sentences, "The device was recovered by SCP personnel following reports of unusual behavior among students at ████ High School in ████, PA. School officials began an investigation when teachers reported that a number of students were behaving unusually." seem redundant. One or the other should be eliminated in my opinion

Anyways, I like this one a lot. Glad to see thought was put into how mental capacity would diminish below a certain size. I do wonder exactly how small a brain could be in real life before it would be impossible for the one with said brain to have human intelligence, however. If anybody finds the exact size (or at least roughly that size), can they edit your article to have the appropriate scale?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very pleased to see how thoroughly and how well this device was thought out. And those images are great - very convincing. +1'ed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for pointing that out. I meant to do a strikethrough on the "or". Presumably the research staff used to be able to approve experiments, but are no longer allowed to do so after the last experiment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the attention to detail. Reminds me of [[SCP-536]].

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor error in the containment description it says approval is need "by the Site Director or and by research staff with Level 3 clearance or higher." (bolding added by me to highlight problem)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That picture is fantastic.

I like the idea that it makes structural changes on-the-fly, as opposed to 'it just gets bigger/smaller', lends it some believability. That and there's plenty of opportunity for further experiments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I rather like this one. But don't cross it with the funnel.

Not sure that it's factory, but eh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very original. The use of images made it very covincing. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a little leery of that last experiment, and I'm inclined to point out similarities to the funnel, though this is more than sufficiently different from that object to stand up on its own. It's also awfully useful; hell, I'd buy one. Fortunately your execution is quite good, and none of what I mentioned is a dealbreaker. I look forward to what we'll see from you in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All good questions. As indicated by the high schoolers mentioned in the Description part of the SCP, serious but non-fatal anomalies generally pop up before anything too fatal. Your question about living versus dead matter is a good one, given that the "function" of the organism is no longer to survive and reproduce.There are literally dozens of interesting experiments you could come up with for the device. Feel free to add your own experiments, provided you adhere to the "rules" established thus far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let's say you repeat Experiments 1056-6 and 7 indefinitely (Shrink, enlarge, rinse and repeat). How long does it take till something fatal comes up?

Also, is there any difference in how living and dead organic parts are treated?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, I've changed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope, groups of interest are free to be used by anyone. We tend to prefer you use them, then make up another, similar group.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought about it. I wasn't sure if The Factory was proprietary to a given author or not - I don't want to step on any toes. If you and others think it's acceptable and would add an overall improvement to this object, I can change it with a quick sentence or two.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad. I think this thing should have a The Factory label, as it seems in line with their work.
